outlines.title: What Is a Fraction?
outlines.description: Introduce fractions with a pizza-slice visual.
keyPoints: A fraction is a part of a whole.
The whole is the full pizza.
A slice is part of that whole.

outlines.title: Numerator and Denominator
outlines.description: Define both halves of the fraction notation.
keyPoints: The numerator is the top number — how many parts you have.
The denominator is the bottom number — total parts in the whole.
Together they tell you 'how many out of how many'.

outlines.title: Reading Fractions Aloud
keyPoints: 1/2 is read as 'one half'.
1/4 is read as 'one quarter'.
3/4 is read as 'three quarters'.

outlines.title: Fractions in Daily Life
outlines.description: Show pizza, chocolate bar, glass of water examples.
keyPoints: A pizza cut in 8 slices and you eat 3 — you ate 3/8.
A chocolate bar with 12 squares — you give 4 to a friend, you have 8/12 left.
A water glass half full is 1/2.

outlines.title: Quick Check: Numerator vs Denominator
outlines.description: 3-question quiz on identifying parts of a fraction.
keyPoints: Identify the numerator in 5/8.
Identify the denominator in 3/7.
Match the fraction to a pizza diagram.

outlines.title: Fraction Builder Simulation
outlines.description: Drag slices to build a fraction visually.
keyPoints: Adjust the denominator to set total parts.
Adjust the numerator to color slices.
Watch the fraction notation update live.

outlines.title: Equal Parts Matter
outlines.description: Why each slice must be the same size.
keyPoints: Fractions only work when the parts are equal.
Three uneven slices is NOT 1/3.
Cutting a pizza into 4 equal slices makes each slice exactly 1/4.

outlines.title: Common Mistakes
keyPoints: 3/4 and 4/3 are very different — order matters.
3/4 is less than a whole; 4/3 is more than a whole.
Always count carefully.

outlines.title: Recap: What We Learned
keyPoints: Numerator on top.
Denominator on bottom.
Fractions need equal parts.

outlines.title: Next Steps
keyPoints: Next we'll add fractions.
Then we'll compare fractions.
Practice helps everything click.

languageDirective: Deliver the entire course in English. Use simple, concrete vocabulary suitable for 4th-grade students. Avoid jargon; introduce one new term at a time with a worked example.
